pirate_significance: Execution Dock at Wapping: Kidd hanged 1701; Admiralty courts and the legal machinery of pirate suppression.
note: Europe's largest city and the hub of Atlantic and East India commerce; home of the South Sea Company, Royal African Company and East India Company.
sources: E.A. Wrigley, 'A Simple Model of London's Importance' (1967)
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic 1675-1740

pirate_significance: Home port of Woodes Rogers; birthplace-by-legend of Blackbeard; outfitting port for privateers.
note: England's second port c.1730 and briefly its leading slave-trading port (1720s-30s) before Liverpool overtook it.
sources: David Richardson, Bristol, Africa and the Eighteenth-Century Slave Trade
Kenneth Morgan, Bristol and the Atlantic Trade

note: Headquarters of the VOC (Amsterdam chamber) and WIC; Europe's financial entrepot; outward Indiamen staged at Texel.
sources: Jonathan Israel, Dutch Primacy in World Trade
Bruijn/Gaastra/Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping

pirate_significance: La Concorde, taken by Blackbeard in 1717 and made Queen Anne's Revenge, was a Nantes slaver.
note: France's leading slave-trading port in the 18th century, dominating the French triangular trade to Saint-Domingue.
sources: Robert Louis Stein, The French Slave Trade in the Eighteenth Century
Jean Mettas, Repertoire des expeditions negrieres francaises

pirate_significance: Terminus of the Brazil fleets whose gold made Portuguese ships prime pirate targets (e.g., Bartholomew Roberts' 1719 Sagrada Familia prize taken from a Lisbon-bound fleet).
note: Capital of a re-enriched empire: Minas Gerais gold flowed through Lisbon's annual Brazil frotas; the pre-1755-earthquake city.
sources: C.R. Boxer, The Golden Age of Brazil 1695-1750

note: The Casa de Contratacion and the American monopoly trade moved here from Seville in 1717; departure/arrival port of the flotas and galeones.
sources: A. Garcia-Baquero Gonzalez, Cadiz y el Atlantico 1717-1778

pirate_significance: Standard first victualling call on the outbound Atlantic track; occasionally used for water and wine by pirate crews.
note: Wine island and near-universal first stop for English ships bound for the Americas; Madeira wine a staple export to the colonies.
sources: David Hancock, Oceans of Wine: Madeira and the Emergence of American Trade (2009)

note: Canary Islands waypoint on the Spanish and general outbound tracks; Canary wine trade; flotas passed via the islands to pick up the NE trades.
sources: Pierre & Huguette Chaunu, Seville et l'Atlantique

pirate_significance: Watering stop on the Guinea and Brazil tracks; the old capital Ribeira Grande was sacked by Jacques Cassard's French squadron in 1712, accelerating the shift to Praia.
note: After the 1712 sack of Ribeira Grande (Cidade Velha), Praia's better-defended bay became the islands' effective port and seat (formally capital in 1770).
sources: T. Bentley Duncan, Atlantic Islands: Madeira, the Azores and the Cape Verdes (1972)

pirate_significance: Home of the 'Sallee Rovers'. The independent corsair republic of Bou Regreg (1627-1668) was long gone by 1730; corsairing continued on a reduced scale under the sultan's license.
note: By 1730 Sale corsairs operated as state-sanctioned privateers of the Alaouite makhzen, a shadow of the early-17th-century peak; still a byword for Barbary captivity in European print (Robinson Crusoe).
sources: Leila Maziane, Sale et ses corsaires 1666-1727 (2007)
Peter Lamborn Wilson, Pirate Utopias

nation_1730: Regency of Algiers (Ottoman, ruled by deys)
pirate_significance: Principal Barbary corsair port: state-organized maritime raiding and ransom economy; treaties with Britain and the Dutch periodically exempted their shipping while smaller nations paid tribute.
note: An autonomous Ottoman regency under the deys; corsairing and ransom remained central but the fleet was smaller than its 17th-century peak.
sources: John B. Wolf, The Barbary Coast: Algiers under the Turks
Daniel Panzac, Barbary Corsairs: The End of a Legend

pirate_significance: Admiralty trials and hangings of Bellamy's survivors (1717) and William Fly (1726, corpse gibbeted); Cotton Mather's gallows sermons.
note: Largest town and busiest port in British North America c.1730; shipbuilding, fish and provisioning trades to the West Indies.
sources: US Census Bureau colonial town estimates
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic

pirate_significance: Pirate-friendly in the 1690s Red Sea era (Thomas Tew sailed from here); 26 of Charles Harris's crew hanged at Gravelly Point in July 1723, one of the largest mass pirate executions in the colonies.
note: Thriving Rhode Island port pivoting from illicit trade toward rum distilling and a growing role in the African slave trade.
sources: US Census Bureau colonial estimates
Marcus Rediker, Villains of All Nations (2004)

pirate_significance: 1690s haven for Red Sea men under Gov. Fletcher (Frederick Philipse financed the Madagascar trade); Kidd's home port. By 1730 respectable.
note: Second port of British North America; flour, provisions and the West Indies trade; the 1730 census counted ~8,600 inhabitants.
sources: Robert C. Ritchie, Captain Kidd and the War against the Pirates
US Census Bureau colonial estimates

note: Fast-growing Quaker port shipping flour and provisions to the Caribbean; would pass Boston in size by mid-century.
sources: US Census Bureau colonial estimates
Gary Nash, The Urban Crucible

pirate_significance: Base of Lt. Robert Maynard's 1718 expedition (sent by Gov. Spotswood) that killed Blackbeard; Blackbeard's head was displayed on a pole at the mouth of the Hampton River ('Blackbeard's Point').
note: Anchorage and convoy rendezvous for the Chesapeake tobacco fleets, the most valuable commodity trade of British America; Royal Navy guardships stationed here.
sources: Robert E. Lee, Blackbeard the Pirate (1974)
Virginia colonial records (Spotswood papers)

nation_1730: Great Britain (Province of South Carolina, royal colony from 1729)
pirate_significance: Blockaded by Blackbeard in May-June 1718 (hostages ransomed for a medicine chest); Stede Bonnet and ~30 others tried and hanged at White Point in late 1718.
note: Rice-boom port and principal town of the southern mainland colonies; heavy slave imports through Sullivan's Island.
sources: Colin Woodard, The Republic of Pirates
Peter Wood, Black Majority

name_1730: Bath and Ocracoke Inlet, North Carolina
nation_1730: Great Britain (Province of North Carolina, royal from 1729)
pirate_significance: Blackbeard took Gov. Eden's pardon at Bath in June 1718, settled there, and used Ocracoke anchorage as his base; killed there in action with Maynard on 22 November 1718 ('Teach's Hole').
note: Tiny capital village and the shoal-guarded inlet serving it; by 1730 a quiet backwater whose pirate decade was over.
sources: Robert E. Lee, Blackbeard the Pirate
North Carolina Office of Archives and History

pirate_significance: Spanish salvage authority over the 1715 wrecks operated from here; the camps were raided by English freebooters (Jennings, 1716), helping seed the Nassau pirate republic.
note: Fortified presidio (Castillo de San Marcos) subsisting on the situado; guarded the Bahama Channel homeward route.
sources: Verne Chatelain, The Defenses of Spanish Florida
Amy Bushnell, The Situado and Sabana

pirate_significance: Bermuda sloops were the fast vessels prized and stolen by pirates; Bermudian wreckers and traders ranged the Bahamas.
note: Maritime economy of fast cedar sloops, salt-raking at the Turks Islands, and carrying trades; key navigational waypoint on the homeward Gulf Stream-Azores track.
sources: Michael Jarvis, In the Eye of All Trade: Bermuda, Bermudians, and the Maritime Atlantic World 1680-1783 (2010)

pirate_significance: Capital of the 'pirate republic' c.1706-1718 (Hornigold, Jennings, Vane, Blackbeard, Anne Bonny). Woodes Rogers arrived July 1718 with the royal pardon, hanged nine holdouts that December, and adopted the motto 'Expulsis Piratis Restituta Commercia'.
note: By 1730 a pacified, poor colonial port living on wrecking, salt and timber; Rogers served a second term as governor (1729-1732) and died there in 1732.
sources: Colin Woodard, The Republic of Pirates (2007)
David Cordingly, Spanish Gold: Captain Woodes Rogers and the Pirates of the Caribbean

pirate_significance: Rendezvous where flotas and galeones combined for the homeward run; base of the guarda costas whose seizures of British ships fed the tensions leading to the War of Jenkins' Ear.
note: The strategic key of the Indies: fortified harbor, and from the 1720s the royal shipyard built major warships of Cuban hardwoods; the doomed 1733 flota sailed from here into the Keys hurricane.
sources: Hugh Thomas, Cuba: The Pursuit of Freedom
AGI records; studies of the Havana arsenal (e.g., G. Douglas Inglis)

pirate_significance: Buccaneer capital of the 1660s-80s (Henry Morgan); two-thirds of the town sank in the 7 June 1692 earthquake. Calico Jack Rackham was gibbeted on a cay off Port Royal (1720); Anne Bonny and Mary Read were tried at Spanish Town.
note: After the 1692 quake, 1703 fire and 1722 hurricane, commerce moved to Kingston (founded 1693), by 1730 Jamaica's commercial hub, while Port Royal remained the Royal Navy station.
sources: Michael Pawson & David Buisseret, Port Royal, Jamaica (1975)
Nuala Zahedieh, 'Trade, Plunder, and Economic Development in Early English Jamaica'

pirate_significance: Major guarda costa / Spanish privateer base preying on British and Dutch shipping in the Windward Passage.
note: Eastern Cuba's port, facing Jamaica across the Windward Passage; copper-mining hinterland (El Cobre).
sources: Richard Pares, War and Trade in the West Indies 1739-1763 (guarda costa background)

pirate_significance: The original buccaneer stronghold of the 1630s-1670s (Le Vasseur's Fort de Rocher, d'Ogeron's governorship). By 1730 long abandoned as a freebooter base — its people had moved to the Saint-Domingue mainland.
note: By c.1730 a thinly peopled adjunct of booming plantation Saint-Domingue; its piratical role was entirely historical.
sources: Alexandre Exquemelin, The Buccaneers of America (1678)
Pierre de Charlevoix, Histoire de l'Isle Espagnole ou de Saint-Domingue (1730-31)

note: Commercial capital of the fastest-growing sugar colony on earth; by 1730 the north-plain sugar boom made Le Cap the richest French American port.
sources: Charlevoix, Histoire de Saint-Domingue (1730-31)
James McClellan, Colonialism and Science: Saint Domingue in the Old Regime

pirate_significance: Chief flibustier (French buccaneer) base of the 1680s — de Grammont and de Graaf sailed from here; seat of early French government in Saint-Domingue.
note: By 1730 eclipsed: government had moved to Leogane and Petit-Goave was a modest sugar and coasting port; its buccaneer generation had been converted to planters after the 1697 Peace of Ryswick.
sources: Charlevoix, Histoire de Saint-Domingue
Philip Boucher, France and the American Tropics

note: Oldest European city in the Americas, by 1730 a poor administrative and ranching outpost overshadowed by the French west of the island.
sources: Frank Moya Pons, The Dominican Republic: A National History

pirate_significance: Base of Miguel Enriquez, the mulatto guarda costa privateer who dominated local waters c.1701-1735 and became one of the richest men in the Spanish Caribbean before his fall.
note: Fortified presidio (El Morro) living on the situado and contraband; Enriquez's corsair fleet made it a real local naval power.
sources: Angel Lopez Cantos, Miguel Enriquez: corsario boricua del siglo XVIII
AGI Santo Domingo records

pirate_significance: Sacked by Pointis and du Casse's buccaneers in 1697 — the buccaneers' last great raid; the treasure galleon San Jose blew up offshore in 1708.
note: Heavily fortified asiento slave port and terminus for the Tierra Firme galeones, serving the Portobelo fairs; rebuilt and strengthened after 1697.
sources: Carla Rahn Phillips, The Treasure of the San Jose
Lance Grahn, The Political Economy of Smuggling (New Granada)

pirate_significance: Sacked by Henry Morgan in 1668 and by Coxon's buccaneers in 1680; its treasure fair was the great prize of the Carrera de Indias.
note: Site of the famous galeones fair exchanging Peruvian silver for European goods; by 1730 the fairs were irregular and withering under South Sea Company competition and contraband (Vernon took the town in 1739).
sources: Geoffrey Walker, Spanish Politics and Imperial Trade 1700-1789
Christopher Ward, Imperial Panama

pirate_significance: The 1683 sack by Laurens de Graaf and Grammont was the most notorious late-buccaneer raid; thereafter heavily guarded.
note: Sole legal Atlantic port of New Spain and terminus of the flota; Mexican silver and cochineal outbound, European goods and mercury inbound.
sources: Chaunu, Seville et l'Atlantique
Garcia-Baquero, Cadiz y el Atlantico

pirate_significance: Repeatedly sacked by buccaneers (1663, 1678, 1685); the half-buccaneer logwood-cutters' nest at nearby Laguna de Terminos was expelled by Spain in 1716-17, pushing the Baymen toward Belize.
note: Walled (from 1704) logwood and provisions port; the logwood frontier conflict with British cutters defined its early-18th-century politics.
sources: Arne Bialuschewski, articles on logwood cutters and piracy in Jamaica/Yucatan
AGI Yucatan records

pirate_significance: Rich sugar shipping made the Barbados approaches favorite pirate cruising grounds; Stede Bonnet, 'the gentleman pirate', was a Barbados planter who turned pirate in 1717.
note: The first great English sugar island's port and one of the largest towns in English America; standard first landfall for ships from Europe and Africa.
sources: Richard Dunn, Sugar and Slaves
Ian K. Steele, The English Atlantic

pirate_significance: Blackbeard captured La Concorde off Martinique in November 1717; French colonial justice here condemned captured pirates.
note: Commercial capital of the French Windward Islands (Fort-Royal was the naval/government seat); sugar and the entrepot trade of the French Antilles.
sources: Philip Boucher, France and the American Tropics
Jean Mettas, Repertoire des expeditions negrieres francaises

note: Premier Dutch entrepot of the Caribbean: free-trading (largely contraband) hub funneling goods and enslaved people into Spanish Tierra Firme.
sources: Wim Klooster, Illicit Riches: Dutch Trade in the Caribbean 1648-1795

note: Fortified island factory off Cap-Vert anchoring French Senegambia trade in gum, gold and enslaved people.
sources: Philip Curtin, Economic Change in Precolonial Africa: Senegambia in the Era of the Slave Trade

nation_1730: Kingdom of Dahomey (conquered from Hueda in 1727); English, French and Portuguese forts on shore
pirate_significance: Namesake of Bellamy's Whydah Gally; Bartholomew Roberts raided the roadstead in January 1722, ransoming ~10 slavers and burning the Porpoise with enslaved people aboard, weeks before his death at Cape Lopez.
note: One of the busiest slave ports in Africa; King Agaja of Dahomey conquered it in 1727, disrupting and then reorganizing the trade under Dahomean control.
sources: Robin Law, Ouidah: The Social History of a West African Slaving 'Port' 1727-1892 (2004)
slavevoyages.org embarkation data

nation_1730: Great Britain (Royal African Company), among Fante polities on the Gold Coast
pirate_significance: After HMS Swallow defeated Bartholomew Roberts at Cape Lopez (Feb 1722), 169 of his men were tried here in the largest pirate trial of the age; 52 were hanged, many in chains along the shore (1722).
note: RAC headquarters on the Gold Coast, exporting gold and enslaved people; flanked by rival Dutch Elmina 13 km west.
sources: A Full and Exact Account of the Tryal of All the Pyrates Lately Taken by Captain Ogle (London 1723)
William St Clair, The Grand Slave Emporium

pirate_significance: Refreshment point that Pirate Round crews had to avoid or bluff past en route to Madagascar; VOC ships were warned here of Indian Ocean pirates.
note: The VOC's mandatory refreshment station ('Tavern of the Seas') between Europe and Asia; a few thousand free inhabitants plus Company servants and enslaved people.
sources: Bruijn/Gaastra/Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping
Nigel Worden et al., Cape Town: The Making of a City

pirate_significance: Bourbon was where retiring Indian Ocean pirates took French amnesties (Condent settled and married there c.1721); Olivier Levasseur ('La Buse') was hanged on Bourbon on 7 July 1730 — the symbolic end of the Indian Ocean pirate era.
note: Coffee colony in full boom under the Compagnie des Indes; Saint-Paul was still the chef-lieu, with Saint-Denis becoming capital in 1738.
sources: Histoire de La Reunion scholarship (J. Barassin; Ho Hai Quang)
Charles Grey, Pirates of the Eastern Seas 1618-1723

nation_1730: France (Compagnie des Indes; claimed 1715, settled from 1721)
pirate_significance: The Dutch abandoned Mauritius (1710) partly because it sheltered pirates; France took it in 1715 largely to deny them the base.
note: In 1730 barely a hamlet: the Compagnie designated Port Louis its chief port c.1731, and only under Mahe de La Bourdonnais (governor from 1735) did it become the famous Indian Ocean naval base — a chronology often misstated on maps.
sources: Auguste Toussaint, Port Louis: deux siecles d'histoire / Histoire de l'Ile Maurice
Megan Vaughan, Creating the Creole Island

nation_1730: Independent Madagascar (Betsimisaraka confederation under Ratsimilaho); no European sovereignty
pirate_significance: THE Indian Ocean pirate haven c.1690-1720: Adam Baldridge's trading post (1691-97) supplied the Pirate Round; Kidd, Halsey, Condent, England and others based or ended here; Adventure Galley and Fiery Dragon lie in its harbor; the 'pirate cemetery' survives. Ratsimilaho, who unified the Betsimisaraka c.1712, was reputedly the son of an English pirate and a Malagasy princess.
note: By 1730 the heyday was over — the last big crews dispersed or took pardons by the mid-1720s — but ex-pirates and their mixed descendants (zana-malata) remained influential; France only gained the island by cession in 1750.
sources: Jan Rogozinski, Honor Among Thieves: Captain Kidd, Henry Every, and the Pirate Democracy in the Indian Ocean (2000)
UNESCO expert mission report, Ile Sainte-Marie (2015)
Arne Bialuschewski, 'Pirates, Slavers, and the Indigenous Population in Madagascar'

pirate_significance: The 'Mocha fleet' of rich Indian pilgrim/trade ships passing the Bab-el-Mandeb was the supreme prize of the Pirate Round (Every's Ganj-i-Sawai 1695; Kidd lurked at the straits in 1697).
note: World's premier coffee port c.1730, with English, French and Dutch factories buying for Europe; coffee shipped from the Yemeni highlands via the Red Sea.
sources: Nancy Um, The Merchant Houses of Mocha (2009)
Robert C. Ritchie, Captain Kidd and the War against the Pirates

pirate_significance: Owner-city of the great hajj ships plundered by Every (1695) and Kidd; European companies in Surat were held answerable for pirate outrages and pressed into convoying Mughal shipping.
note: Still a great Mughal commercial port c.1730 but declining as Mughal authority waned and Bombay rose; English, Dutch and French factories present.
sources: Ashin Das Gupta, Indian Merchants and the Decline of Surat c.1700-1750 (1979)

pirate_significance: The EIC's Bombay Marine waged constant war on Kanhoji Angre, the Maratha admiral the English branded a 'pirate', whose fleet dominated the Konkan coast until his death in 1729.
note: EIC headquarters for western India since 1687; fortified harbor city with a growing dockyard (Parsi Wadia master-builders engaged from 1736).
sources: K.N. Chaudhuri, The Trading World of Asia and the English East India Company
Studies of Kanhoji Angre and the Bombay Marine (e.g., Manohar Malgonkar, Kanhoji Angrey)

note: Capital of the Portuguese Estado da India, much decayed; under Maratha pressure that would cost it the Province of the North (Bassein) in 1737-39.
sources: C.R. Boxer, The Portuguese Seaborne Empire 1415-1825

pirate_significance: EIC shipping from Coromandel was a Pirate Round target in the 1690s; Governor Thomas Pitt fought the trade with the Madagascar men.
note: EIC's chief settlement on the Coromandel coast, exporting cotton textiles; a thriving dual 'white town / black town' city.
sources: K.N. Chaudhuri, The Trading World of Asia
H.D. Love, Vestiges of Old Madras

nation_1730: Great Britain (East India Company), under Bengal Nawabi suzerainty
note: Founded 1690; by 1730 the booming EIC gateway to Bengal textiles and saltpetre, fast becoming the company's most profitable trade.
sources: K.N. Chaudhuri, The Trading World of Asia
P.J. Marshall, Bengal: The British Bridgehead

note: The VOC's Asian capital and rendezvous of all its shipping; c.1730 increasingly malarial (beginning its 'graveyard of the East' decades) yet still the greatest European city in Asia.
sources: Bruijn/Gaastra/Schoffer, Dutch-Asiatic Shipping
Leonard Blusse, Strange Company: Chinese Settlers, Mestizo Women and the Dutch in VOC Batavia

pirate_significance: Terminus of the Manila galleon — the single richest ship afloat and the dream prize of every raider (Rogers took the Encarnacion off Cabo San Lucas in 1709; Anson took the Covadonga in 1743).
note: Entrepot exchanging American silver for Chinese silks and porcelain via the annual Acapulco galleon; the Chinese (Sangley) merchant community ran much of the trade.
sources: W.L. Schurz, The Manila Galleon (1939)

note: By the 1720s-30s European China trade (tea, porcelain, silk) had concentrated at Canton, with ships mooring downriver at Whampoa; the formal 'Canton System' monopoly dates from 1757 but the pattern was set by 1730.
sources: Paul Van Dyke, The Canton Trade: Life and Enterprise on the China Coast 1700-1845 (2005)

pirate_significance: Galleon terminus whose silver fair enticed every South Sea raider of the age.
note: Sleepy fortified bay (Fuerte de San Diego) that exploded into life for the annual galleon fair, then emptied; goods packed overland to Mexico City.
sources: W.L. Schurz, The Manila Galleon

pirate_significance: Old Panama burned after Morgan's 1671 raid; the new walled city (founded 1673) anchored the Pacific end of the silver portage that 1680s buccaneers crossed the isthmus to raid.
note: Pacific terminus of the Portobelo fair route; transshipment of Peruvian silver across the Camino Real.
sources: Christopher Ward, Imperial Panama: Commerce and Conflict in Isthmian America
Peter Earle, The Sack of Panama

pirate_significance: Base of the Armada del Mar del Sur guarding silver shipments; in the raid zone of South Sea intruders (Rogers 1709, Clipperton and Shelvocke 1719-21).
note: Lima's fortified port shipping Potosi silver north to Panama; destroyed by the great 1746 earthquake-tsunami (after our date).
sources: Peter Bradley, The Lure of Peru: Maritime Intrusion into the South Sea 1598-1701 (and 18th-c. sequels)
Geoffrey Walker, Spanish Politics and Imperial Trade

pirate_significance: Captured and ransomed by Rene Duguay-Trouin's French squadron in 1711 (after Duclerc's failed 1710 raid) — the biggest amphibious prize-taking of the era.
note: Boom port of the Minas Gerais gold rush; the gold frota sailed annually to Lisbon; it replaced Salvador as capital only in 1763.
sources: C.R. Boxer, The Golden Age of Brazil 1695-1750

pirate_significance: Bartholomew Roberts' first exploit as captain (1719) was cutting out the rich Sagrada Familia from the Lisbon-bound fleet in the Bay of All Saints.
note: Capital of Brazil until 1763 and one of the largest cities in the Americas; sugar, tobacco and the Mina slave trade.
sources: C.R. Boxer, The Golden Age of Brazil
Stuart Schwartz, Sugar Plantations in the Formation of Brazilian Society

note: Pernambuco's sugar port, recently riven by the Guerra dos Mascates (1710-11) between Recife merchants and Olinda planters; elevated to town status in 1709.
sources: C.R. Boxer, The Golden Age of Brazil

note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: fast-rising third English slave port in the 1730s, soon to overtake Bristol; completes the British triangular-trade picture.
sources: David Richardson, Suzanne Schwarz & Anthony Tibbles (eds.), Liverpool and Transatlantic Slavery

pirate_significance: Successor to Petit-Goave as the colony's seat; many ex-flibustier families settled the plain.
note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: seat of French colonial government of Saint-Domingue c.1730, between Petit-Goave's decline and Port-au-Prince's founding (1749).
sources: Charlevoix, Histoire de Saint-Domingue (1730-31)

note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: Dutch WIC headquarters on the Gold Coast, 13 km from Cape Coast Castle; the oldest European building in sub-Saharan Africa.
sources: Harvey Feinberg, Africans and Europeans in West Africa: Elminans and Dutchmen on the Gold Coast

note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: the single largest slave-embarkation port in Africa, feeding Brazil; essential for any slave-route layer.
sources: slavevoyages.org embarkation totals
Joseph Miller, Way of Death: Merchant Capitalism and the Angolan Slave Trade

pirate_significance: France's premier corsair (privateer) port — Duguay-Trouin's home; Malouin interlopers ran the illicit South Sea (Peru) trade c.1700-1724.
note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: corsair wealth and the Compagnie des Indes connection make it the French counterpart to Bristol.
sources: Andre Lespagnol, Messieurs de Saint-Malo: une elite negociante au temps de Louis XIV

note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: headquarters of the French Compagnie des Indes in India, flourishing under Governor Lenoir c.1730; balances the EIC ports.
sources: Philippe Haudrere, La Compagnie francaise des Indes au XVIIIe siecle

note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: Portuguese gateway to the China trade and pilot station for Canton-bound ships; the standard European landfall in the Pearl River approaches.
sources: C.R. Boxer, Fidalgos in the Far East
Paul Van Dyke, The Canton Trade

note: SUGGESTED ADDITION: South Sea Company asiento factory and the great contraband back door toward Potosi silver; documents the southern Atlantic route.
sources: Geoffrey Walker, Spanish Politics and Imperial Trade 1700-1789
Zacarias Moutoukias, studies of Buenos Aires contraband
